title: Data Update 2 for 2026: Equities get tested, and pass again!
gist: Damodaran shows US equities returned 17.72% in 2025 despite tariff shocks and political turmoil, argues the historically high PE ratio is offset by resilient earnings growth and record buybacks, and pegs the implied equity risk premium at 4.23% - roughly average, not bubble territory - while admitting he personally has kept idle cash in T-bills rather than redeploying it.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 rose from 5881.6 to 6845.5 in 2025, a 16.39% price gain, plus a 1.34% dividend yield, for a total return of 17.72%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Confirms 2025 was a genuinely strong year for US equities despite a news backdrop that felt uniformly negative.
insights.locator: Back from the Brink: US Equities in 2025

insights.claim: 2025's 17.72% return ranked 45th of 98 years of US equity returns from 1928-2025 (near the median), and marked the third consecutive year the annual return exceeded the median - the longest such streak since the mid-1990s.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A three-year above-median streak is historically rare and is one of the bear case's own talking points (mean reversion is 'due'), so investors should watch whether 2026 breaks or extends the streak.
insights.locator: Across the entire year, the S&P 500 rose

insights.claim: US equities returned 85.32% cumulatively from 2023 through 2025, a period when many market timers were advising investors to stay out of the market entirely.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that bearish market-timing calls over the last three years cost investors an 85% cumulative return - a caution against acting on similar 2026 bear calls.
insights.locator: US equities between 2023 and 2025

insights.claim: Stocks sold off sharply and hit their 2025 low on April 11, after a 'Liberation Day' tariff announcement whose breadth and magnitude blindsided markets in early April, before mounting a comeback that leveled off by late September.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Shows markets can absorb even a shock-magnitude policy surprise (tariffs) within months, a data point for gauging resilience to future policy shocks.
insights.locator: The sell off was brutal and short

insights.claim: Communication services (which includes Alphabet and Meta) was the best-performing sector by percentage return in 2025 at 30.63%, followed by technology at 23.65%, but technology added the most in dollar terms with $4.17 trillion of market cap growth.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Tech/communication services drove the bulk of 2025's dollar wealth creation, reinforcing how narrow the market's dollar-value leadership actually was.
insights.locator: The best performing sector in percentage returns

insights.claim: Consumer staples and real estate were the worst-performing sectors in 2025, returning only about 2% for the year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Defensive sectors materially lagged the broad market, signaling the year's rally was not a flight-to-safety move but a growth/momentum-driven one.
insights.locator: The worst performing sectors were consumer staples

insights.claim: Precious metals companies were the single best-performing industry group in 2025 with a 169.2% return, driven by the surge in gold and silver prices; other energy and mining names also ranked among the top performers.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A 169% industry return signals that the gold/silver rally translated into extreme equity outperformance for miners, far outpacing broad equity gains.
insights.locator: The surge in gold and silver prices in 2025

insights.claim: The worst-performing industries in 2025 were concentrated in old-economy businesses: chemicals, consumer product companies, and food processing all struggled.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Old-economy defensive/staples-adjacent industries were the clearest laggards, consistent with the sector-level staples weakness.
insights.locator: The worst performing businesses were primarily old economy

insights.claim: The small-cap premium and the value premium - two well-documented 20th-century return anomalies - have been fading or disappearing in the 21st century.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors still building portfolios around classic small-cap or value factor tilts are fighting a structural, multi-decade trend, not a temporary dip.
insights.locator: the fading or even disappearance of two well documented phenomena

insights.claim: Small-cap and low price-to-book (value) stocks partially returned to favor in 2025, but Damodaran cautions against celebrating this reversal, expecting these groupings to trade off winning in some years and losing in others rather than resume sustained outperformance.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A tempered forecast for factor investors: expect episodic, not durable, outperformance from small-cap/value going forward.
insights.locator: If you are small cap or a value investor

insights.claim: The Mag Seven's collective market capitalization rose 22.36% in 2025, but performance diverged sharply within the group: Apple and Amazon posted only single-digit gains while Nvidia rose 37.8% and Alphabet rose 62.7%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The 'Mag Seven' is no longer a monolithic trade; dispersion within the group means stock selection inside it now matters as much as owning the basket.
insights.locator: While the Mag Seven saw their collective market capitalization increase by 22.36%

insights.claim: The Mag Seven's share of the roughly 6,000-stock US equity market grew from 11% in 2014 to 30.89% at the end of 2025, and the market cap they added in 2025 accounted for 39.3% of the entire increase in US equity market capitalization that year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Roughly 40 cents of every dollar of 2025 US equity market-cap growth came from just seven stocks - a concrete measure of concentration risk for index investors.
insights.locator: The aggregate market cap of the Mag Seven has increased

insights.claim: Damodaran states the 'Mag Seven party' will undoubtedly end at some point, but it did not end in 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A standing but unfulfilled prediction of eventual reversal in mega-cap tech dominance - useful as a recurring checkpoint each year this thesis doesn't play out.
insights.quote: While this Mag Seven party will undoubtedly end at some point, it did not happen in 2025.
insights.locator: While this Mag Seven party will undoubtedly end

themes.theme: The Overvaluation Debate: PE Ratios vs Fundamentals

insights.claim: By every PE ratio measure (trailing, normalized 10-year average earnings, and Shiller/inflation-adjusted 10-year), the S&P 500's valuation at the start of 2026 is higher than at almost any extended period in history, with the possible exception of the late 1990s.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The headline valuation metric bears cite is factually at or near record highs - the key question is whether that alone should trigger de-risking.
insights.locator: Using every PE ratio measure

insights.claim: Damodaran argues the PE ratio is a noisy and unreliable timing indicator, noting that using it to call US stocks overpriced would have kept an investor out of US equities for much of the last decade.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Directly undercuts the most common bear argument for 2026; investors should be skeptical of PE-based market-timing calls given their poor recent track record.
insights.locator: The problem with the PE pricing metric

insights.claim: US corporate earnings grew about 356% since 2000 (as the S&P 500 rose from 1320.28 to 6845.5), and there have been only three years this century of earnings declines: 2001 (dot-com bust), 2009 (2008 financial crisis), and 2020 (Covid).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Structural earnings resilience is offered as the primary fundamental justification for elevated valuations, not just multiple expansion - watch whether 2026 introduces a fourth down-earnings year.
insights.locator: Robust Earnings Growth & Earnings Resilience

insights.claim: S&P 500 companies returned more than a trillion dollars in cash via buybacks alone in 2025, and while the pure dividend payout ratio has fallen all century, an 'augmented cash yield' combining dividends and buybacks is comparable to historical dividend yields from before the buyback era.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reframes the shareholder-return story: cash returns haven't shrunk, they've shifted form to buybacks - investors judging yield only by dividends are understating true cash return to shareholders.
insights.locator: Healthy cash returns

insights.claim: The bear case for 2026 rests on stocks having had three good years in a row, history showing stocks generally underperform in the second year of a US presidential term, and PE ratios pushing toward historic highs.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Lays out the specific, checkable bear arguments circulating at the start of 2026 that an investor can track against actual 2026 performance.
insights.locator: there seems to a decided tilt towards bearishness

insights.claim: Damodaran's implied equity risk premium (ERP) methodology treats the index level and expected cash flows (dividends and buybacks, grown at the earnings growth rate) as inputs to an internal-rate-of-return calculation; subtracting the riskfree rate from that expected return yields the ERP, a market-derived, model-agnostic number.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives investors a reproducible way to judge market pricing that incorporates rates, growth, and cash flows together rather than relying on PE alone.
insights.locator: The implied equity risk premium, as I compute it

insights.claim: As of January 1, 2026, with the S&P 500 at 6845.5, Damodaran computes an expected return on stocks of 8.41%; subtracting the T-bond rate of 4.18% yields an implied equity risk premium of 4.23%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This 4.23% ERP is the article's central 'fair value' anchor for judging whether the S&P 500 is over- or under-priced entering 2026.
insights.locator: I obtain an expected return on stocks of 8.41%

insights.claim: Sensitivity analysis shows a 2% equity risk premium implies an S&P 500 value of 14834 (53% undervalued at current levels), while a 6% ERP implies a value of 4790 (43% overvalued).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Quantifies exactly how sensitive 'fair value' for the index is to ERP assumptions - a 4-point ERP swing moves fair value by roughly 3x, underscoring how much of any bull/bear call is really a bet on the risk premium, not earnings.
insights.locator: With a 2% equity risk premium

insights.claim: The current implied ERP is almost exactly equal to the historical average for the 1960-2025 period, though it is lower than the post-2008 average; Damodaran distinguishes this from a bubble, noting the ERP fell to just 2.05% at the end of 1999.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts 'bubble' framing for the current market by contrasting today's ERP with the genuinely extreme 1999 reading, while still conceding bears have a case versus the post-2008 average.
insights.locator: I am leery of the bubble word

insights.claim: Damodaran, who describes himself as a non-market-timer, admits he has held back on putting idle cash into US equities over the last year, preferring to keep it in treasury bills.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A notable tell: even a stated non-timer has been de facto underweighting equities via cash allocation, suggesting genuine unease about current pricing despite his own valuation framework not screaming 'overvalued'.
insights.quote: I have held back on putting idle cash back into US equities in the last year, preferring to keep that cash in treasury bills.
insights.locator: I am, at my core, a non market-timer

insights.claim: Damodaran states the healthiest outcome for the market in 2026 would be a return roughly equal to its expected return of 8-9%, allowing valuation excess to correct gradually through time rather than through a price crash.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete benchmark (8-9% return) against which to judge whether 2026 is 'healthy' normalization versus renewed excess or a sharp correction.
insights.locator: the healthiest development for the market would be

insights.claim: For the bear case to be validated, Damodaran suggests 2026 would need to be the year tariffs and political whiplash finally hit corporate earnings and cash flows, though he notes waiting on the sidelines for that to happen has not been a good strategy for the last decade.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific catalyst (tariff/policy pass-through to earnings) that would need to materialize for bears to finally be right, after a decade of being wrong.
insights.locator: For the bears, this may also be the year

insights.claim: Damodaran flags that there are signs of excess in some parts of the market, especially related to AI, even as he argues the broader market is not in bubble territory.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Signals a bifurcated view: aggregate-index valuation looks defensible, but AI-linked pockets of the market warrant specific scrutiny for excess.
insights.locator: there are signs of excess in at some parts of the market
